course_description:
This course covers key theories, models, approaches and concepts relating to human lifespan development, from conception to the end of life. It examines the application of this knowledge within social work, also considering the phenomena of cultural diversity and disability. Students will learn to support human wellbeing by identifying and leveraging available sources of strengths and resilience as people adapt and seek to cope with the risks and adversities they may experience throughout their lives.

Welcome to SWSP7044: Lifespan, Culture, and Disability!

SWSP7044 examines the application of knowledge of human development to social work practice. The course aims to help students develop an understanding of the multidimensional nature of human development. It introduces issues concerning the nature of development across the lifespan, including (a) understanding and critically engaging with major concepts and theories of human development,ﾠ(b) understanding the factors that affect human development and considering these in terms of risks for well-beingﾠand sources of resilience, and (c) exploring the implications thereof for social work practice.

Social work is informed by theoretical frameworks that regard personalities, behaviours, choices and actions within theﾠcontexts in which they occur. Sound knowledge of human growth and development is a valuable tool for understanding people. It enables an appreciation, at once, of people’s innate potentials and capabilities, the challenges they may encounter over a lifetime, and the complex factors that may aid or hinder development, adaptation to changing life circumstances, resilience in the face of stress and trauma, and their well-being over time. Additionally, there has been a time-honoured belief within social work that people are unique. Thus, whatever their field of practice, social workers must account for these individual differences. All this enables social workers to help create conditions in which healthy growth and adjustment may occur, to help people address and overcome challenges and constraints, and to increase their options as they navigate typical transitions, developmental tasks, and crises throughout their lifespan.

This course provides the necessary foundational knowledge for students to understand the bio-psycho-social functioning of individuals as well as the variability in human identity, behaviour, preferences, and choices, with due consideration of contextual constraints and enablements. Social workers applyﾠtheir knowledge when communicating with others, making assessments, and planning interventions in a variety of contexts. Throughout the semester, students will develop these practice skills by applying their growing knowledge of human development to a complex case.

I look forward to working with you this semester.

course_contacts.name: Dr Dorothee Hölscher
course_contacts.notes: By arrangement. If you wish to make an appointment, please chat with me after class or email me so we can arrange an in-person or online appointment.ﾠﾠﾠ

UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Weekly lectures begin in Week 1.
Tutorials begin in Week 2.
All students are requested to sign into a tutorial by Week 1 of the semester.
course_aims: The aims of this course are to:

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and describe key individual and contextual features, events, and processes in human development.
Critically evaluate a range of theories related to human development.

Investigate historical and cultural influences in human development, specifically regarding their impact on (a) Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and (b) culturally and linguistically diverse migrant communities.
Analyse the concept of disability and examine how varying interpretations influence human development across the lifespan.
Apply a human development framework to conduct assessments, considering risks, adversities, and sources of resilience to support wellbeing across the lifespan.
Integrate critical reflexivity and cultural humility when conducting assessments within a human development framework.
Exhibit advanced written and oral communication skills and demonstrate effective independent and group collaboration abilities.

assessment_summary.due_date: 24/04/2026 - 14/05/2026; PowerPoint slides are due in Week 8 on Friday, 24 April 2026 at 1:00 pm;; Presentations will be conducted during the Weeks 9-11 tutorials.

assessment_details.title: Quiz (in-class)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This in-class/in-person quiz is an initial task to assess your knowledge of the foundational material covered during Weeks 1 to 3 of this course. Your quiz is designed as a closed-book assessment containing 40 multiple-choice questions worth 0.5 marks each . The quiz is scheduled for Wednesday, 25 March 2026 at 12:00 pm , and must be completed within 40 minutes (plus 10 minutes reading time). As this quiz is taken in class/in person, you must attend the lecture period during which it is scheduled to be able to complete this task . Note: Full details about the use of AI and MT for assessment tasks in this course are available on the course Blackboard site. It is essential that students familiarise themselves with those requirements. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
Please refer to the guidelines above and see the notes below.
Deferring the quiz :
Deferral requests are submitted online via mySI-net. Deferral requests received in any other way will not be approved. When submitting your request please choose type mid-semester exam (not end of semester ). Please note that SAPs are not acceptable evidence to apply for a deferred examination.
Disability Policy Statement :
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements in the subject is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Services. The University Health Service can arrange advice and assistance on professional accreditation/registration issues that might arise as a result of alternative arrangements.

assessment_details.title: Group Presentation (in-class) - Case Analysis
assessment_details.due_date: 24/04/2026 - 14/05/2026 PowerPoint slides are due in Week 8 on Friday, 24 April 2026 at 1:00 pm; Presentations will be conducted during the Weeks 9-11 tutorials.
assessment_details.task_description: In Week 5, students will be allocated to groups for their second assessment task: Group presentation (In-class) – Case analysis . The relevant case study will be published at the start of Week 5. PowerPoint slides are due at the end of Week 8, and presentations are conducted in person during tutorial time during Weeks 9, 10, and 11. This task requires students to work in groups of three to four to prepare and present a professional conversation about the case study provided, including your consideration of how best to support the main character in coping with past and current adversities, risks, and difficulties, promoting their strengths, and enhancing their resilience. The conversation will be maximally 7.5 minutes per group member, that is, a total of maximally 22.5 minutes for a group of three students and 30 minutes for a group of four. Your presentation will be supported with PowerPoint slides, to which all members are expected to contribute. Note: Full details about the use of AI and MT for assessment tasks in this course are available on the course Blackboard site. It is essential that students familiarise themselves with those requirements. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
ONE nominated group member is required to submit the PowerPoint slides no later than Friday, 24 April 2026, 1:00 pm , via the Blackboard submission point provided.
Your PowerPoint slides must include the following: the presentation title, names and student numbers of group members, an outline of presentation content, and references. Referencing must be in APA 7th (see Guides on the UQ Library website). There is no minimum or maximum number of references; however, using high-quality academic sources is expected.
These slides (submitted in Week 8) will be used in your group presentations (scheduled in Weeks 9 to 11 during tutorial times). Students registered for the online tutorial will be expected to attend their presentation in-person.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Please note: Extensions are only available for PowerPoint slide submissions .
If you require an extension for the slides, you must ensure that your group members have been consulted and that the Group Acknowledgement Form is completed as a team. Only the group member who is sick or experiencing unavoidable circumstances that meet the extension criteria needs to submit the extension request , provided all group members have been consulted. If you are unable to present with your group on the scheduled day and time, please contact your course coordinator directly , as extensions are not available for the presentation.
Applying for an extension
Extension requests are submitted online via my.UQ. Extension requests received in any other way will not be approved.
Extension guidelines
Extension requests should be submitted prior to the assessment deadline. Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date may not be approved. Late requests must include evidence of the reasons for the late request as per the guidelines in my.UQ . The extension granted should be in proportion to the period of illness or disruption caused by the exceptional circumstances and will typically be no more than the specified maximum extension length for each assessment. A maximum of three assessment requests can be submitted for each assessment, and the third request must include an Assessment Management Plan. Students who have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 4 weeks in a semester are advised to carefully consider whether they are capable of successfully completing their courses and may need to consider applying for removal of course . Students who have a disability or a chronic medical condition (including a mental health condition) are encouraged to meet with a Disability Advisor within Student Services to request a Student Access Plan (SAP) with a clause on extension requests. Students who have an SAP can apply for a 7-day extension.
Outcome of application
Extension requests are generally processed within 2 working days.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Exam: Quiz and Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description: This final assessment task (Exam - Quiz and Case Analysis) is a closed-book assessment with materials permitted of 180 minutes (plus 10 minutes reading time). It is an opportunity for you to demonstrate your knowledge and understanding of human development as a multidimensional process and to show how it may inform social work’s contribution to enhancing human wellbeing in line with “principles of social justice, human rights, collective responsibility, and respect for diversity” (IFSW/IASSW 2014). To this end, your end-of-semester exam will be based on a case study, provided in Week 12, and require you to answer a set of four short-answer questions, one short essay question, and 20 multiple-choice questions. Note: Full details about the use of AI and MT for assessment tasks in this course are available on the course Blackboard site. It is essential that students familiarise themselves with those requirements. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 180 minutes Calculator options No calculators permitted Open/closed book Closed book examination - specified written materials permitted Materials An unmarked bilingual dictionary is permitted. Materials to be supplied to Students : 1 x 14-Page Answer Booklet. 1 x Multiple Choice Answer Sheet Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to Lifespan Development / Dimensions of human development This introductory lecture provides the foundation for the contents covered in the remainder of the semester. It comprises three parts: An overview of the course and its relevance to social work practice, including a discussion of the structure, content and assessment in the course. An introduction to Harms' (2021) multidimensional approach to human development as an overarching framework to locate, critique, and apply the theories and debates covered in this course. We also consider the importance of locating ourselves critically and reflexively in relation to the contents covered in this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Introduction to the concept of culture In this lecture, we examine the concept of culture and its relevance for understanding human development. We explore the intersections between the relational, social, structural, and cultural contexts of people's lives, focusing on the concepts of culture, cultural humility, safety, and responsiveness. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Discussion, application, and critique / Assessment 1 preparation In Week 2, we commence with our weekly two-hour tutorials. This week, we - Critically discuss the relevance, strengths and limitations of contemporary conceptualisations of culture, cultural humility, responsiveness, and safety, and their links with trauma-informed practice. Support your preparations for your first assessment task (in person/in-class quiz scheduled for Week 5). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Stages of human development / Erikson's theory of psychosocial development In our Week 3 lecture, we focus on the stages of human development, as conceptualised by Erik Erikson's in his theory of psychosocial development. We will consider: The origin, main tenets, applications, and critiques of Erikson's theory of psychosocial development. How the idea of developmental stages contributes to organising this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Discussion, application, and critique / Asessment 1 preparation In Week 3, we continue with our weekly two-hour tutorials. This week, we - Critically discuss the relevance, strengths and limitations of Erik Erikson's theory of psychosocial development. Support your preparations for your first assessment task (in person/in-class quiz scheduled for Week 5). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Introduction to the concept of disability For Week 4, we invited a guest lecturer, A/Prof Kathy Ellem, to conduct a two-hour seminar to help us examine the contested nature of health and ability. We explore definitions and models of disability and reflect on attitudes and biases impacting the health and development of people with disability. Our theoretical focus is on Dan Goodley's disability studies matrix. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

Revision of Weeks 1-4 content and final Assessment 1 preparation In our Week 4 tutorials, we - Revise the Weeks 1-4 content. Complete our preparations for your first assessment task (in person/in-class quiz scheduled for Week 5). Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Quiz (in-class) - During lecture time This in-person/in-class quiz is held during your regular lecture date and time. The venue will change, and this will be announced closer to the date. The quiz contains 40 multiple-choice questions, covering contents from Weeks 1 to 4 of this course to test your understanding of the theoretical concepts framing this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

The prenatal period / Attachment theories After the quiz, we commence our lectures on human development across the lifespan, focusing on the prenatal period and introducing attachment theories. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Publication of Case Study for Assessment 2 The case study upon which your second assessment task (case analysis) will be based is made available after you have completed your quiz, in time for our weekly tutorials .

Discussion, application, and critique / Assessment 2 preparation begins In this week's tutorials, we - Address any open questions concerning our lecture and online content. Critically discuss the origin, main tenets, applications, and points of critique of attachment theories. Set up your your Assessment 2 groups. Discuss the case study upon which your second assessment task will be based, as well as work through some of the common challenges in group-based assignments, including how best to resolve them. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Infancy and early childhood development / Piaget's theory of cognitive development This lecture focuses on the developmental tasks and transitions in infancy and early childhood, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during these stages of the lifespan. Our theory in focus is Jean Piaget's theory of cognitive development. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Discussion, application and critique / Assessment 2 preparation continues In this week's tutorial groups, we - Address any open questions concerning our lecture and online content. Critically discuss the origin, main tenets, applications, and points of critique of Piaget's theory of cognitive development. Continue your Assessment 2 preparations with an in-depth discussion of our marking rubric - also in relation to the case study provided and lecture contents from the previous weeks. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Development in middle childhood / Vygotsky's socio-cultural theory of development This lecture examines the developmental tasks and transitions in middle childhood, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during this stage of the lifespan. Theory in focus: Lev Vygotsky's sociocultural theory of cognitive development. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Discussion, application, and critique / Assessment 2 prep continues In this week's tutorial groups, we - Address any open questions concerning our lecture and online content. Critically discuss the origin, main tenets, applications, and points of critique of Vygotsky's socio-cultural theory of development. Continue your Assessment 2 preparations with an opportunity to raise any questions and concerns that have come up in your group work - also in relation to the case study and lecture contents from the previous weeks. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Development in adolescence / Kohlberg's theory of moral development This lecture examines the developmental tasks and transitions in adolescence, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during this stage of the lifespan. Our theoretical focus is on Lawrence Kohlberg's theory of moral development. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Discussion, application, and critique / Final assessment 2 preparation In this week's tutorial groups, we - Address any open questions concerning our lecture and online content. Critically discuss the origin, main tenets, applications, and points of critique of Kohlberg's theory of moral development. This is your final opportunity to ask and discuss any remaining questions about your second assessment task before your group presentation slides are due and group presentations commence. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
PowerPoint slides are due for all groups. Group presentation slides are due on the Friday of Week 8 at 13:00. Group presentations are held through Weeks 9 to 11, during tutorial times.

Development in young adulthood / Arnett's theory of emerging adulthood This lecture focuses on the developmental tasks and processes associated with young adulthood, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during this period of development. Our theory in focus is Arnett's theory of emerging adulthood. What does this recently proposed stage of emerging adulthood entail, and how does it compare to both adolescence and young adulthood? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Group presentations begin Case Analysis. Students only need to attend the slots allocated for their group presentations. Students in the online tutorial group: Please remember and plan for coming to campus at your allocated times, as your group presentations, too, need to be held face-to-face. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Development in middle adulthood / Levinson's model of adult development This lecture explores the developmental tasks and transitions in middle adulthood, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during this stage of the lifespan. our theoretical focus is on Levinson's model of adult development. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Group presentations continue Case Analysis. Students only need to attend the slots allocated for their group presentations. Students in the online tutorial group: Please remember and plan for coming to campus at your allocated times, as your group presentations, too, need to be held face-to-face. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Development in late adulthood / Baltes' tenets of lifespan development This lecture focuses on the developmental tasks and transitions in middle adulthood, as well as key sources of risk, adversity, and resilience during this stage of the lifespan. Our theoretical focus is on Paul Baltes tenets of lifespan development. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Final round of group presentations Case Analysis. Students only need to attend the slots allocated for their group presentations. Students in the online tutorial group: Please remember and plan for coming to campus at your allocated times, as your group presentations, too, need to be held face-to-face. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
Publication of case study for the exams The case study upon which your exam (your second case analysis) will be based is made available at the start of Week 12.

Special topic: Displacement, migration, and human development In this seminar, a mental health social worker with almost 20 years of experience in international social work and one of their service users talk about practice with refugees and asylum seekers and how people may navigate life challenges under conditions of displacement and resettlement. We use this opportunity to return to the topics of culture, cultural humility, safety, and responsiveness to consider their links with trauma-informed practice in social work. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Assessment 3 (exam) preparation In this week's tutorial groups, we - Focus on your exam preparations with an in-depth discussion of the examiner's expectations. Address any open questions concerning our lectures and online content of the previous weeks. Have an opportunity to critically discuss the theories discussed in the preceding weeks: In terms of origin, application, and points of critique, what are the similarities and differences, the overlaps and contradictions between Harms' multidimensional approach, Levinson's model, and Baltes' tenets of lifespan development? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Special topic: Social work at the end of life While grief and loss are themes that pervade all social work practice, at the end of the semester, in this two-hour seminar, we want to consider the relevance of these themes for the end of life and for those left behind. To this end, we have invited a social worker from Queensland Health with years of experience in emergency care and cancer services to talk about her practice in supporting patients and their loved ones in coping with different forms of loss and grief. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Assessment 3 (exam) preparation This is your final opportunity to ask and discuss any remaining questions about your exams before the study break and the Sem. 1/2026 examination period begins. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
